transcript: I wanted to share my five-step algorithm that I use in the clinic when I'm answering the phone now I have a clinical team I have an administrative team so I'm not usually the person answering the phone but one of the most important elements of success in private practice is developing systems that allow any person on your team to produce extraordinary results for the activity at hand we're going to talk about answering the phone we follow a five-step algorithm step number one when the phone rings before anyone in the clinic answers the phone they look at the caller ID they want to see is this a patient that's expected to come into the clinic in the next 10 minutes on the phone if it is we can safely assume that patient's probably calling because there's a conflict they're trying to reschedule they're trying to cancel is it a name that we don't recognize but a name of an individual we can safely assume that's probably somebody calling to schedule a new appointment we're going to approach that very differently is it an insurance company calling on the phone is it a Spam call is it a call for something that's just going to waste our time uh so step number one is we look to see who's calling step number two if we recognize the name on the phone so let's say it's 9:47 in the clinic and my 10:00 patient is the person calling when I see that I immediately go into my reschedule mode I look at my schedule even before I answer the phone I usually have my schedule always open on a computer there's computer next to every phone in the clinic so what I'm looking for is if my 10:00 person needs to reschedule can I reschedule them for the same day can I reschedule them for another day during the same week if it's a Monday or a Tuesday there's a chance I can get them on Wednesday Thursday Friday if they're not already scheduled for that day I want to then think if I can't get them scheduled for the same day I can't get them scheduled for another day on the same week is there an opportunity for me to bump up the frequency for the subsequent week now that's something that I could address over the phone right now while they're calling or it's something I could address when we're face Toof face in the clinic I would tell you you probably have a better chance of getting them in for an additional day next week when you're face Toof face in the clinic because that's when you can have an honest conversation you can better understand their response or their reaction to what it is that you're recommending now once I've gone through those three steps try to reschedule for the same day try to reschedule for an additional day that week or try to add them on for an additional day in the following week then I start to look at my schedule to see is there anyone else I can move into the now vacant spot if I have individuals that are doing non-covered maintenance in the clinic are any of those individuals appropriate for a covered visit title: 10 Essential Tips for Medical Receptionists: Mastering Daily Operations!
author: Simply Motsoka
transcript: Hey everyone and welcome back to the channel. Welcome to Simply Matsoka Medical Insight Slot. Today we're diving into the critical role of the medical receptionist. Your role is more than just answering phones and scheduling. You are the calm in the storm and the organizational backbone. Let's explore essential tips to master your role and make a difference. Think of this as your practical guide to excelling in your position. So, grab a notepad, get comfortable, and let's get started. Let's make every day at the practice a success story. All right, let's kick things off with tip number one. Always start your day with a morning briefing. A quick, focused huddle with your team, even if it's just for 10 to 15 minutes, can set the tone for the entire day. This proactive approach helps everyone get on the same page, anticipate challenges, and support each other [Music] effectively. Tip number two, and this one is absolutely non-negotiable. Keep the appointment diary meticulously up to date in real time. Every appointment made, canceled, or rescheduled needs to be entered accurately and immediately. An outdated diary can lead to double bookings, missed appointments, long patient wait times, and a whole lot of frustration for both patients and staff. When a patient calls to reschedule, update it instantly. This precision is a hallmark of a top tier medical receptionist. Moving on to tip number three, confirm appointments in advance. Proactively reaching out to patients a day or two before their scheduled visit significantly reduces no shows and last minute cancellations. A gentle reminder call, text, or email not only helps patients remember their commitment, but also gives them an easy opportunity to reschedule if needed. This proactive communication shows patients that you're organized and that their appointment is valued, enhancing their overall experience even before they step into the [Music] practice. Chapter 4, practice effective time management. Prioritize tasks based on urgency and importance. Batch similar tasks together to minimize context switching. Use digital calendars, to-do lists, and reminder apps to stay organized and efficient. Chapter 5. Maintain clear and compassionate communication. Clear communication means providing information accurately and concisely. Compassion is about empathy. Putting yourself in the patients shoes. Acknowledge their feelings, listen actively to their concerns, and respond with kindness and understanding. This blend of clarity and compassion builds trust and fosters a positive environment in the [Music] clinic. Chapter six, organize patient records and files daily. Ensure all new patient information is entered correctly and existing records are updated promptly. Process and file all paperwork and digital entries before the end of your shift. Your diligence and recordkeeping directly contributes to the quality of care patients receive and the overall operational integrity of the practice. Chapter 7 focuses on a core responsibility. Manage incoming calls professionally. Answer promptly with a clear, friendly greeting stating the practice name and your name. Active listening is crucial. Pay close attention to the caller's needs or questions. Professionalism on the phone conveys competence and care, making callers feel valued and confident in your practice services. Our eighth tip delves into an area requiring precision and trust. Monitor and reconcile daily finances. Carefully process all payments, issue receipts clearly, and accurately record each transaction. At the end of the day, reconcile your collections to ensure financial accountability. This daily reconciliation provides an accurate financial snapshot for the practice. Tip number nine is a mindset more than a task, but it's essential for thriving as a medical receptionist. Stay calm and adaptable. Take a deep breath, assess the situation, and prioritize your actions. Adaptability goes handinand with calmness. This resilience ensures the practice continues to function as smoothly as possible no matter what. And finally, tip number 10. End your day with a summary and prepare for tomorrow. Review what was accomplished. Note any unresolved issues and ensure all financial reconciliations are complete. Tidy your workspace, restock supplies, and prepare any paperwork or schedules needed for the next day. This end of day routine minimizes morning chaos and contributes to a consistently wellorganized and prepared front desk environment. So, there you have it. Essential tips to excel as a medical receptionist. You are the directors of first impressions, the navigators of patient flow, and the keepers of calm and order. title: Are You Employing Physiotherapists
author: College Of Physiotherapists of Ontario
transcript: Do you have a physiotherapist working for you or your clinic? It’s important to know that physiotherapy is a self-regulated profession with a central governing body called the College of Physiotherapists of Ontario. Our job is to license physiotherapists and oversee the rules that they’re required to follow. We also investigate complaints from the public. As an employer, there are things you need to do to make sure both you and your employee are meeting these accountabilities: First, check the Public Register to make sure the person you’re employing is officially registered. This is where you can verify their name, registration number and conduct history. You can also see their certificate of registration. This tells you whether your employee requires supervision or if they can practice on their own. The College has rules covering things like working with physiotherapist assistants, fees, billing, and what needs to be included in an invoice. It’s important that you support your employee in following these rules. Here are a few to keep in mind: All physiotherapists must have liability insurance. Your employee can tell you what’s required. Physiotherapists need to roster to perform high risk activities like acupuncture. This means completing an online process verifying that they’re qualified before performing the activity on a patient. This information appears on the Public Register. You should also know that, as an employer, the law requires you to report certain things to the College – this is called mandatory reporting. Understanding these obligations will build a strong working relationship between you and your employee, and most importantly, create a safe environment for patients. Contact our Physiotherapy Information Advisor with any questions or have a look at the employer section of our website at collegept.org.
